narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 35, high family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 71.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your classmates and stood out well below the typical student's average decile of about 5.7, signaling early academic struggles that strongly correlated with bar exam challenges in the model's analysis. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile of 3, again lower than the average of 5.5, which reinforced concerns about consistent performance even as you progressed. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, compared to the dataset average of 3.21, added further weight toward the failure prediction, though its influence was smaller than your deciles. While your LSAT was close to average and your high family income and full-time status were neutral or slightly positive, they couldn't offset the top concerns from your grades and rankings. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher deciles and GPAs indicating stronger academic trajectories, your profile aligned more closely with those who faced bar exam hurdles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar Hispanic communities succeed with targeted improvements—but the model focused on these measurable academic signals. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your upper-year performance through tutoring or study groups, and perhaps gaining practical experience like internships to build a more competitive profile. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the prediction in your favor, and schools often value resilience in applicants like you. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a supportive family background positions you well for a comeback.
